explicit_text: I was born at Wesley Hospital in Oklahoma City, which later became Presbyterian Hospital. When I had a serious accident at age 7, my parents rushed me back to Wesley Hospital for treatment. The care I received there was critical, especially since I had severe head injuries and had to keep my head on the pillow for eight weeks. Wesley Hospital played a big role in my early life and recovery.

That hospital played a big role in my early life and recovery.

explicit_text: Oklahoma City is where I grew up and where many important events in my life took place. My parents met here after moving from Texas during the Great Depression. I attended schools like Gatewood Elementary, Cleveland Grade School, Taft Junior High, and Classen High School all in Oklahoma City. I also spent a lot of time at Holland Field and Texas League Park watching the Oklahoma City Indians play baseball.

This economic hardship shaped how my parents met and eventually settled down. It was a tough time but also a turning point for my family.

explicit_text: The Oklahoma City Indians were my favorite baseball team growing up. My dad’s grandmother’s great-uncle owned half the team, so I got free tickets and even pitched batting practice to the players. I was at every game at Holland Field and Texas League Park, watching future major leaguers like Al Rosen and Paul Easterling. The Indians were a big part of my childhood and love for baseball.

explicit_text: I vividly remember December 7, 1941, the day of the Pearl Harbor attack. I was 10 years old and playing outside with my neighbor when we heard about the airplane that might be bombing us. I went inside to find my dad listening anxiously to the Philco radio trying to understand what was happening. That day marked a turning point in my childhood and the start of the U.S. involvement in World War II.
